metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: EFP Spoken Language 10 centers on First Peoples oral traditions and the art of public speaking grounded in Indigenous communication practices. Students explore storytelling protocols, ceremonial language, and the cultural significance of oral communication in Indigenous communities. Through studying First Peoples speakers and orators, students develop skills in public speaking, podcasting, and oral performance while honoring traditional oral narrative structures and contemporary Indigenous voices. This course emphasizes the power of spoken language to convey meaning, build community, and maintain cultural knowledge.
metadata.general_description: An exploration of Indigenous oral traditions, public speaking, and spoken word grounded in First Peoples communication practices and values.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous oral traditions are sophisticated, deliberate communication systems that convey knowledge, maintain relationships, and transmit cultural wisdom across generations.
big_ideas.elaboration: First Peoples oral communication includes specific protocols about when and how stories should be shared, who can tell certain narratives, and what responsibilities accompany storytelling. Oral traditions incorporate rhythm, repetition, call-and-response patterns, and community participation—creating living, dynamic systems of knowledge transmission that adapt while maintaining cultural integrity.

big_ideas.statement: Spoken language is a powerful tool for asserting Indigenous identity, sharing perspectives, and challenging injustice through oratory, poetry, and public discourse.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous speakers, poets, activists, and educators use spoken language to articulate worldviews, document experiences, educate audiences, and mobilize communities. Contemporary Indigenous oratory draws on traditional eloquence while addressing modern issues, proving that oral communication remains vital for Indigenous presence and power in public life.

big_ideas.statement: Effective oral communication requires attention to tone, rhythm, pacing, audience awareness, and cultural context to convey meaning and create connection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Speaking skills extend beyond words to include vocal qualities, silence, physical presence, and responsiveness to audience. Indigenous communication styles emphasize relationship-building, respect, and appropriate contextualization of messages. These elements work together to ensure meaning is conveyed accurately and respectfully.

big_ideas.statement: Podcast and audio media provide contemporary platforms for Indigenous storytelling, education, and voice that extend oral traditions into digital spaces.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous podcasters, musicians, and audio creators use modern technology to share stories, interview elders and community members, and disseminate Indigenous knowledge widely. Audio platforms preserve the vocal and sonic qualities essential to oral traditions while reaching audiences beyond geographic and temporal boundaries.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect
categories.raw: Students listen to and analyze Indigenous oral communication, understanding protocols, techniques, and cultural meanings embedded in spoken language.

competencies.raw: Analyze oral narratives and storytelling techniques used by Indigenous speakers and storytellers
i_can_statements: I can identify storytelling protocols, structures, and techniques in Indigenous oral narratives
I can explain how Indigenous speakers use rhythm, repetition, pacing, and vocal qualities to convey meaning
I can recognize the role of silence, pauses, and audience interaction in Indigenous oral communication
I can discuss how oral narratives differ from written versions in meaning and impact

competencies.raw: Understand the cultural protocols and responsibilities associated with Indigenous storytelling and oral communication
i_can_statements: I can explain who has the authority to tell certain Indigenous stories and why these protocols matter
I can describe the responsibilities that accompany receiving or sharing Indigenous oral knowledge
I can identify how cultural context shapes the meaning and appropriate use of Indigenous stories
I can discuss the significance of community participation in Indigenous oral traditions

competencies.raw: Analyze contemporary Indigenous speakers, poets, and orators, understanding how they use spoken language for education, advocacy, and cultural affirmation
i_can_statements: I can identify key Indigenous speakers, poets, and public figures and analyze their rhetorical strategies
I can explain how Indigenous orators address social justice issues and assert Indigenous rights through spoken word
I can analyze the impact of Indigenous podcasts, audio stories, and oral performances on audiences
I can discuss how contemporary Indigenous communication honors traditional oral traditions

categories.name: Create and Communicate
categories.raw: Students develop and deliver their own oral presentations, speeches, and spoken performances drawing on Indigenous communication practices.

competencies.raw: Prepare and deliver speeches, presentations, and oral performances with attention to audience, purpose, and cultural appropriateness
i_can_statements: I can plan and deliver speeches or presentations with clear purpose and appropriate structure
I can use vocal techniques (tone, pacing, emphasis) effectively to convey meaning and engage audiences
I can adapt my communication style to different audiences and contexts
I can demonstrate cultural awareness and respect when presenting on Indigenous topics

competencies.raw: Create and perform spoken word, poetry, or storytelling grounded in Indigenous narrative traditions
i_can_statements: I can write and perform spoken word or poetry that incorporates Indigenous storytelling elements
I can craft narratives that honor oral tradition structures while expressing contemporary perspectives
I can perform stories with attention to rhythm, pacing, and audience engagement
I can reflect on how my spoken performances demonstrate understanding of Indigenous communication practices

competencies.raw: Produce podcast episodes, audio recordings, or oral presentations that share Indigenous knowledge, stories, or perspectives
i_can_statements: I can plan and record podcast episodes or audio stories from Indigenous perspectives
I can interview elders, community members, or Indigenous experts respectfully and authentically
I can edit and produce audio content with attention to sound quality and storytelling impact
I can articulate the purpose and intended audience for my oral/audio projects

areas.name: First Peoples Oral Traditions and Protocols
areas.raw: Study of Indigenous storytelling traditions, protocols governing who tells stories and how, and the significance of oral communication in First Peoples cultures
topics: Traditional storytelling protocols and responsibilities
Creation stories and teaching stories in Indigenous cultures
Seasonal storytelling practices and ceremonial language
The role of elders and knowledge keepers in oral traditions
Variations across different First Peoples nations and communities
How oral traditions maintain cultural identity and transmit values

areas.name: Indigenous Speakers and Oratory
areas.raw: Analysis of Indigenous orators, speakers, poets, and public figures who use spoken language for education, advocacy, and cultural expression
topics: Historical and contemporary Indigenous orators and their rhetoric
Indigenous spoken word poetry and performance
Indigenous activists and educators speaking for social justice
Indigenous leaders articulating sovereignty, rights, and self-determination
Indigenous artists and performers using spoken language creatively
Regional and nation-specific oratory traditions and famous speakers

areas.name: Oral Communication Skills and Techniques
areas.raw: Development of effective public speaking, presentation, and oral performance skills with attention to voice, presence, engagement, and cultural context
topics: Vocal techniques: tone, pitch, pacing, volume, and emphasis
Physical presence and non-verbal communication in speaking
Audience awareness and responsive communication
Structuring oral presentations and speeches effectively
Storytelling techniques for engaging and moving audiences
Managing nervousness and building confidence in public speaking

areas.name: Indigenous Podcasts, Audio, and Digital Oral Media
areas.raw: Exploration of Indigenous-produced podcasts, audio stories, and digital audio media that extend oral traditions into contemporary formats
topics: Indigenous-produced podcasts and their themes and impact
Audio storytelling and recorded oral narratives
Indigenous music, hip-hop, and spoken word recordings
Interviewing and documentary audio practices
Podcast production and audio editing techniques
How digital platforms preserve and share Indigenous oral knowledge
